title: LFPT5: A Unified Framework for Lifelong Few-shot Language Learning Based on Prompt Tuning of T5

structured.summary: The paper proposes LFPT5, a unified prompt tuning framework built on T5 for lifelong few-shot language learning across multiple NLP tasks. It introduces a novel approach using prompt embeddings as both task solvers and data generators to mitigate forgetting via pseudo sample rehearsal and KL divergence regularization, demonstrating improved performance and parameter efficiency over strong baselines.

strengths.point: Unified framework for multiple task types in lifelong few-shot learning
strengths.evidence: LFPT5 handles sequence labeling, classification, and generation tasks by reformulating them into a text-to-text format compatible with T5.

strengths.point: Parameter-efficient continual learning via prompt tuning
strengths.evidence: Only small prompt embeddings (~0.04% of T5 parameters) are tuned per task, keeping the backbone frozen, reducing risk of forgetting and enabling scalability.

strengths.point: Innovative use of prompt embeddings as both task solvers and data generators
strengths.evidence: Pseudo samples generated from learned prompts are used to rehearse previous tasks, combined with KL divergence loss to enforce label consistency.

strengths.point: Comprehensive empirical evaluation across diverse NLP tasks and datasets
strengths.evidence: Experiments on NER, classification, and summarization with few-shot setups show LFPT5 outperforms fine-tuning, regularization-based methods, and adapter baselines.

strengths.point: Open sourcing code to facilitate reproducibility and further research
strengths.evidence: The authors release their codebase, supporting transparency and community adoption.

weaknesses.point: Lack of comparison to recent prompt-based lifelong learning baselines
weaknesses.evidence: No evaluation against methods like L2P or DualPrompt that also use prompt pools for continual learning, limiting assessment of novelty and relative gains.

weaknesses.point: Insufficient analysis of pseudo sample generation hyperparameters and quality impact
weaknesses.evidence: No ablation on number or quality of pseudo samples or decoding strategies, leaving sensitivity and robustness unclear.

weaknesses.point: Sparse details on few-shot setup and reproducibility
weaknesses.evidence: Unclear how few-shot samples are selected (random or balanced), no mention of multiple seeds, and missing prompt initialization and length details.

weaknesses.point: Evaluation metrics lack forgetting curves and per-task performance over time
weaknesses.evidence: Only average final scores reported, which do not fully demonstrate forgetting mitigation or continual learning dynamics.

weaknesses.point: Limited discussion on scalability and domain/task diversity
weaknesses.evidence: Experiments cover up to 10 tasks but no analysis on scaling to many tasks or handling out-of-distribution or highly diverse tasks.

weaknesses.point: Ambiguity in prompt interaction and terminology
weaknesses.evidence: Unclear how task-specific prompts combine or interact during inference; dual role of prompts as solvers and generators not clearly distinguished.

questions.question: How does LFPT5 perform compared to recent prompt-based lifelong learning methods like L2P or DualPrompt?
questions.motivation: Understanding relative advantages over state-of-the-art prompt-based continual learning is critical to assess novelty and practical impact.

questions.question: How sensitive is LFPT5 to the number and quality of generated pseudo samples, and what decoding strategies optimize rehearsal effectiveness?
questions.motivation: Clarifying these factors is important for reproducibility and for improving forgetting mitigation in lifelong few-shot learning.

questions.question: Can LFPT5 scale efficiently to a large number of tasks or domains, and how does prompt storage and inference complexity grow?
questions.motivation: Scalability is key for real-world lifelong learning applications involving many tasks over time.

questions.question: Does LFPT5 generalize to other model architectures beyond T5 and to languages other than English?
questions.motivation: Demonstrating broader applicability would strengthen claims of general lifelong few-shot learning capability.

critique_points.point_001: Lack of comparison to recent prompt-based lifelong learning baselines. No evaluation against methods like L2P or DualPrompt that also use prompt pools for continual learning, limiting assessment of novelty and relative gains
critique_points.point_002: Insufficient analysis of pseudo sample generation hyperparameters and quality impact. No ablation on number or quality of pseudo samples or decoding strategies, leaving sensitivity and robustness unclear
critique_points.point_003: Sparse details on few-shot setup and reproducibility. Unclear how few-shot samples are selected (random or balanced), no mention of multiple seeds, and missing prompt initialization and length details
critique_points.point_004: Evaluation metrics lack forgetting curves and per-task performance over time. Only average final scores reported, which do not fully demonstrate forgetting mitigation or continual learning dynamics
critique_points.point_005: Limited discussion on scalability and domain/task diversity. Experiments cover up to 10 tasks but no analysis on scaling to many tasks or handling out-of-distribution or highly diverse tasks
critique_points.point_006: Ambiguity in prompt interaction and terminology. Unclear how task-specific prompts combine or interact during inference; dual role of prompts as solvers and generators not clearly distinguished
